coredata.dc:title: Low cost fabrication of continuous flow optofluidic microreactor for efficient dye degradation using graphene QDs@MOF (Ti) photocatalyst
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Verma, Gulshan
Venkatesan, Prashanth
Roy, Deblina
Aloni, Parthivi
Dega, Naresh Kumar
Gupta, Ankur
coredata.dc:description: The optofluidic microreactor, a convergence of optics and microfluidics, offers advanced functionalities that can be pivotal in the rapid assessment of nanocatalysts for tackling environmental contamination issues. This article presents an efficient approach for degrading Methylene blue (MB) dye, commonly used in the textile industry, within a cost-effective polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) based continuous flow optofluidic microreactor. This microreactor combines graphene quantum dots (QDs) and NH2-MIL-125 (MOF(Ti)) as a highly effective photocatalyst coating within its microchannels. By directly incorporating graphene QDs@MOF(Ti) into the microchannels, the photocatalytic medium is brought into close proximity with the flowing MB dye solutions, thereby reducing the necessary interaction time and enhancing purification efficiency. Furthermore, the findings demonstrate an impressive degradation efficiency of ∼99% for MB dye at a flow rate of 50 μL min−1 under visible light irradiation, achieved in a single pass. Additionally, the microfluidic reactor exhibits prolonged stability of the photocatalyst, enabling its reuse without significant efficiency loss. In addition, a comparative analysis highlights the advantages of microreactor-based photocatalysis over traditional methods. These advancements in the features of the graphene QDs@MOF(Ti) nanocomposite substantiate their demonstrated superiority in degradation efficiency.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Optofluidic microreactor
Graphene quantum dots
Metal organic framework
MOF (Ti)
Efficient photocatalysis
Methylene blue degradation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of both MOF(Ti) and graphene QDs@MOF(Ti) photocatalyst

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The author is pleased to acknowledge Ministry of Education (MoE), India, IIT Jodhpur for financial assistantship, and Environmental Chemistry & Catalysis Laboratory (National Tsing Hua University, Taiwan) for experiments and Data Analysis. The author also thanks Ms. Sakshi Bhagat (Department of Chemistry, IIT Jodhpur) for assisting in NMR characterization of untreated and treated MB dye samples.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic image for the synthesis of MOF(Ti) and graphene QDs.
(A) FESEM images of MOF(Ti), (B) TEM image of graphene QDs, (C–D) HRTEM images of graphene QDs and graphene QDs@MOF(Ti), (E–F) EDS and elemental mapping of graphene QDs@MOF(Ti).
(A) XRD and (B–F) XPS spectrum of MOF(Ti) and graphene QDs@MOF(Ti).
(A–B) BET and pore size distribution, (C–D) UV–Vis and Tauc plot, (E–F) photocurrent and Nyquist plot for both MOF(Ti) and graphene QDs@MOF(Ti).

(A–C) UV–Vis spectra for degradation of dye using (A) MOF(Ti), (B) 2-graphene QDs@MOF(Ti), (C) 4-graphene QDs@MOF(Ti) at different flow rate under visible light exposure respectively, (D) comparative analysis of degradation efficiency, (E–F) experimental and kinetics study results for all combination of photocatalyst.
The mechanism of MB dye degradation using graphene QDs@MOF(Ti) composite.
Chemical structure and 1H NMR spectra of MB dye before and after degradation.
A comparison with the previously published composite based on MOF(Ti) for dye degradation.

head.ce:title: Low cost fabrication of continuous flow optofluidic microreactor for efficient dye degradation using graphene QDs@MOF (Ti) photocatalyst

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Jodhpur, 342030, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Jodhpur 342030, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Analytical and Environmental Science, National Tsing Hua University, Hsinchu City, 30013, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Analytical and Environmental Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Analytical and Environmental Science, National Tsing Hua University, Hsinchu City 30013, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research and Development, Dalko Diagnostics Pvt Ltd. Hyderabad, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research and Development, Dalko Diagnostics Pvt Ltd. Hyderabad, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The optofluidic microreactor, a convergence of optics and microfluidics, offers advanced functionalities that can be pivotal in the rapid assessment of nanocatalysts for tackling environmental contamination issues. This article presents an efficient approach for degrading Methylene blue (MB) dye, commonly used in the textile industry, within a cost-effective polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) based continuous flow optofluidic microreactor. This microreactor combines graphene quantum dots (QDs) and NH2-MIL-125 (MOF(Ti)) as a highly effective photocatalyst coating within its microchannels. By directly incorporating graphene QDs@MOF(Ti) into the microchannels, the photocatalytic medium is brought into close proximity with the flowing MB dye solutions, thereby reducing the necessary interaction time and enhancing purification efficiency. Furthermore, the findings demonstrate an impressive degradation efficiency of ∼99% for MB dye at a flow rate of 50 μL min−1 under visible light irradiation, achieved in a single pass. Additionally, the microfluidic reactor exhibits prolonged stability of the photocatalyst, enabling its reuse without significant efficiency loss. In addition, a comparative analysis highlights the advantages of microreactor-based photocatalysis over traditional methods. These advancements in the features of the graphene QDs@MOF(Ti) nanocomposite substantiate their demonstrated superiority in degradation efficiency.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, the growth of industries combined with rising populations, has resulted in the release of toxic contaminants in the water bodies (Saucier et al., 2015). This increase in pollutants has led to a significant risk for both the environment and humanity. These contaminants even in low concentrations, can affect the essential aquatic life cycles by blocking light and oxygen from entering the water (Liu et al., 2017; Prashanth et al., 2021). Additionally, textile contaminants such as dyes are considered to be one of the harmful toxins that are difficult to break down under natural conditions (Gupta et al., 2015). For example, Methylene blue (MB) dye based wastewater is extensively released pollutants from textile industries. Therefore, there is an essential requirement for advanced methods for eliminating these toxins from water sources. The literature reports the use of different methods for treating these toxins via techniques such as electrocoagulation (Rai et al., 2022), chemical oxidation, and adsorption (Anh Tran et al., 2021), etc. However, these methods are often unable to fully eliminate these modern pollutants; instead, they relocate the dye molecules rather than removing them (Rai et al., 2023; Verma et al., 2023). For instance, electrocoagulation frequently requires significantly higher cost and energy, and generates a substantial amount of sludge, thus contributing to the increase of secondary pollution and waste management issues. Similarly, chemical oxidation is an expensive method that results in incomplete dissociation of dyes and produces toxic byproducts. Furthermore, in the adsorption method, the dye is merely relocated in another form, necessitating an additional disposal process. In contrast, advanced technology such as photocatalysis is important, as it alters the dye molecules into harmless substances, effectively addressing the issue (Verma et al., 2024). Consequently, it presents a more efficient, sustainable, and environmentally friendly solution.

Additionally, with the advancement of technology, the use of optofluidic microreactors are increasingly utilized for wastewater treatment, as this technique combines the use of optics, microfluidics, and chemical reactions in compact yet smaller devices (Rambabu et al., 2022). As compared to traditional methods, these techniques have a higher surface-to-volume (S/V) ratio, which improves the photoinduced process (Parmar et al., 2015). Further, the presence of short transfer length along with uniform light distribution enables the spontaneous chemical reactions, thus improving the energy density within the microreactor (Li et al., 2014). The performance of the photocatalysis technique is determined by two factors: photocatalysis efficiency (light adsorption and charge transport within catalyst) and mass transfer efficiency (the interaction of catalyst and reactants, and removal of resultant byproducts) (Kim et al., 2022a). Additionally, the optofluidic microreactor is a cost-effective technology that requires only a minimal amount of photocatalyst to be coated on the internal channels, yet it achieves results comparable to conventional methods. Due to the small volume of solution that flow continuously in the microreactor's channels, light penetration is uniform across the reactor's depth, enabling rapid degradation of contaminants often within few minutes, whereas traditional reactors may take several hours (Das and Srivastava, 2016). Despite their swift reaction rates, microreactors face challenges in meeting the demands of wastewater treatment facilities due to their limited throughput, which hinders their commercial potential. To address this issue, increasing the number of microchannels within a single device or utilizing parallel microchannel reactors can enhance operational efficiency and make the technology suitable for large-scale industrial use. Consequently, this advanced approach serves as a practical strategy to solve the limitations of traditional treatment methods, advancing waste purification technology to higher levels of enhanced effectiveness.

In recent years, the researcher has increased focus on designing photocatalysts in various 0D to 3D nanostructures, to maximize their S/V ratio to significantly enhance their photocatalytic degradation efficiency (Chauhan et al., 2019; Gupta et al., 2023; Verma and Gupta, 2022; Wu et al., 2020). For example, titanium (Ti) based metal organic framework (MOF) materials with their unique features such as porous structure, customizable pore characteristics, and enhanced surface area, emerges as a suitable adsorption material for removal of organic dyes from wastewater (Roy et al., 2023; Yao et al., 2023). These porous structures not only enable them for excellent adsorption and diffusion but also enhance light trapping, resulting in the abundance of active sites (Bůžek et al., 2021; Zhu et al., 2016). Further, graphene quantum dots (QDs) offer a large surface area and excellent electron transfer capabilities, which help reduce the recombination of electron/hole (e−/h+) pairs and improve charge separation, while also expanding the photocatalyst's light absorption into the visible range (Ibarbia et al., 2020; Lu et al., 2015). Moreover, the combination of graphene QDs and the MOF(Ti) composite has not yet been extensively explored for textile dye degradation. This novel composite is anticipated to offer enhanced electron mobility, broader light absorption, and more efficient generation of reactive oxygen species, all of which are expected to significantly accelerate and improve the degradation of MB dye.

The article aimed to demonstrate the fabrication of low cost optofluidic microreactor with graphene QDs@MOF(Ti) photocatalyst decorated microchannel for effectively degrading MB dye. The intricate porosity of the MOF(Ti) serves as a crucial facilitator, augmenting the dispersion and stability of graphene QDs, thereby supporting their effectiveness throughout the degradation process. Moreover, the integration of graphene QDs enhances the efficient transfer of photogenerated electrons (e−), thereby amplifying the composite's degradation capabilities. These advancements in the features of the graphene QDs@MOF(Ti) composite substantiate their demonstrated superiority in degradation efficiency.

Citric acid (C6H8O7), 2-Aminoterephthalic acid (NH2-BDC; C8H7NO4); dimethylformamide (DMF; C3H7NO), methanol (CH3OH), titanium butoxide(C16H36O4Ti), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), slygard 184 and curing agent were procured from Sigma Aldrich. All solutions were prepared in DI water with a high purity of 18.2 MΩ-cm.

The continuous flow microreactor is fabricated on PDMS using double replication technique. Initially, the acrylic sheet (thickness: 4 cm) is ultrasonically clean using DI water. Subsequently, the design is precisely patterned on the acrylic sheet (master mold) using a laser micromachining technique. The pattern is then transferred to the PDMS. To achieve this, Slygard 184 and curing agent are mix in 10:1 and pour on the pattern acrylic sheet (Sheshkar et al., 2022). Followed by degassing in vacuum desiccator to remove trap air bubble and finally curing at 50 °C for 3 h. After curing, the PDMS is carefully peel off from the acrylic master, serving as a negative master for the subsequent PDMS double replication process. The PDMS negative master is then coated with a 10 nm of gold NPs, which acts as an anti-adhesive surface for the second PDMS mixture. Following the pouring of PDMS mixture, the mold is then cured at 50 °C for 3 h. Once cured, the positive PDMS pattern is peeled off and thermally bonded to a glass slide. Lastly, inlet and outlet holes are punched using a plunger, and tubes are attached to finalize the microreactor's assembly.

The MOF(Ti) was synthesized through a solvothermal reaction. The process commenced with the ultrasonication of 6 mM C8H7NO4 in a mixture of C3H7NO and CH3OH, each 3.5 ml. Subsequently, 1.5 mM (0.5 ml) of C16H36O4Ti was mixed with the prepared solution and further ultrasonicated for 5 min. The resulting solution was then shifted to a Teflon-lined autoclave and subjected to a 16 h reaction at 150 °C, resulting in the formation of a yellowish precipitate. Post-reaction, the resulting precipitate was centrifuged and washed with C3H7NO to remove any unreacted organic ligands, followed by additional washing with CH3OH. Finally, the product was dried under vacuum conditions to obtained MOF(Ti) powder (see Fig. 1 ).

The graphene QDs was synthesized through a direct pyrolysis method using citric acid. The procedure involved adding 2 g of C6H8O7 into a 200 mL beaker. The beaker was then heated to 200 °C, to start the process of liquification of C6H8O7, which gradually turned to yellowish color over a 5 min. After 35 min of heating, an orange hue emerged, indicating the formation of graphene QDs. Subsequently, the resulting orange liquid, containing graphene QDs, was carefully added drop by drop into a vigorously stirred solution of 10 μM NaOH. Following pH adjustment to 8 using NaOH, the graphene QDs solution underwent dialysis in a 1k Da dialysis bag for 24 h, with dialysate replacement every 8 h, to eliminate residual chemicals. The purified solution of graphene QDs was then freeze dried for 24 h.

The graphene QDs@MOF(Ti) composite is prepared in two steps. First, 15 mg of MOF(Ti) powder is dissolved in DI water in two containers separately. Then, 2 ml and 4 ml of graphene QDs solution (0.2 mg/mL in DI) are added to the respective containers. The obtained solutions undergo 12 h of ultrasonication, after which they are centrifuged and rinsed with DI water. Finally, it was collected after being dried at 80 °C for 5 h to obtain 2-graphene QDs@MOF(Ti) and 4-graphene QDs@MOF(Ti) composites, respectively.

The photocatalytic performance of both MOF(Ti) and graphene QDs@MOF(Ti) was assessed by examining the MB dye degradation. Specifically, 0.01 g of each photocatalyst was dissolved separately in acetone. These photocatalyst solutions were then passed through the microchannel at a rate of 50 μL min−1 to uniformly coat the interior of the channel at 50 °C. Finally, a 100W Xe lamp served as the visible light source for the experiment, positioned at a distance of 15 cm from the microreactor. The schematic view of the final optofluidic microreactor is depicted in Fig. 2 . For the experiment, a stock solution of 10 μM MB dye in DI water was prepared and subjected to continuous stirring and left in darkness for 30 min to reach absorption-desorption equilibrium before the start of the experiment under visible light. The degradation efficiency of prepared photocatalysts was evaluated by varying the flow rate of the MB dye solution (500, 200, 150, 100, and 50 μL min−1) in the microchannel. The treated dye solution is then collected and analyzed in UV–Vis spectrum (400–700 nm) to examine the relative absorbance of MB dye. As per the Beer-Lambert law, which establishes a linear correlation between concentration and absorbance, the normalized absorbance corresponds to the normalized concentration of the MB solution. Previous research has consistently indicated that the degradation kinetics of MB dyes follows Langmuir-Hinshelwood kinetic model. Furthermore, equations (1)–(3) provides the formula to calculate the degradation efficiency (E%), and rate constant (k) (Verma et al., 2022).
(1)
E
( % )
=
(
1 −
C
C 0
)
× 100
(2)
−
d C
d t
= − k A
(3)
ln
C
C 0
= k t + c o n s t a n t
Where C
0 corresponds to the initial concentration of dye as it enters the microreactor, while C represents the final concentration of dye after undergoing the degradation process.

The morphological characteristics and elemental distribution of MOF(Ti) and graphene QDs@MOF(Ti) were meticulously analyzed using Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FESEM, Thermo-fisher scientific), Energy-dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDX; CARL ZEISS and SDD JEOL Centurio), and Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM; JEOL JEM-ARM200F). Further the crystalline structure and chemical composition were analyzed through X-ray Diffractometry (XRD; Bruker D8) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; Ulvac-PHI 1600), respectively. Additionally, the surface area and porosity were precisely quantified using Micromeritics ASAP 2020. The 1H NMR spectra of both untreated and treated MB dye solutions were captured at 500 MHz using a Bruker Ascend WB NMR Spectrometer (Germany). D2O served as the solvent for dissolving the prepared MB solution for NMR analysis, with chemical shifts expressed in δ (ppm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of both MOF(Ti) and graphene QDs@MOF(Ti) photocatalyst
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of MOF(Ti) was examined using FESEM imaging (see Fig. 3 (A)). The FESEM images revealed that MOF(Ti) exhibited a truncated square bipyramid shape with an average size of ∼531 nm. Furthermore, the morphology of graphene QDs, synthesized through the pyrolysis of citric acid, was analyzed using TEM (see Fig. 3(B)). Further, the particle size histogram, shown in the inset of Fig. 3(B), indicates an average size of 3.985 ± 1.3 nm. Moreover, the Raman spectra of graphene QDs are depicted in Fig. S1, revealing two distinct peaks at 1350 and 1590 cm−1, which are attributed to the D and G bands, respectively. The D band is indicative of structural imperfections and disorder within the graphene QDs, whereas the G band is associated with the in-plane vibrations of sp2 bonded carbon atoms. These bands are characteristic of graphene-based materials, providing strong evidence for the formation of graphene QDs. The detailed morphological assessment of graphene QDs and graphene QDs@MOF(Ti) is further investigated using a HRTEM technique as shown in Fig. 3(C and D). The HRTEM depicts distinct lattice fringes characterized by a spacing of ∼0.24 nm, which corresponds to the (1120) lattice planes of graphene. This observation provides additional evidence supporting the claim that the synthesized QDs exhibit graphene-like characteristics. These lattice fringes are also present in the graphene QDs@MOF(Ti) composite, suggesting that the graphene QDs were successfully integrated into the MOF(Ti) framework. To confirm the coexistence of graphene QDs with MOF(Ti), EDS and elemental mapping analysis were performed (Fig. 3(E and F)). The EDS spectrum and elemental mapping images revealed a homogeneous distribution of carbon (from the graphene QDs) along with Ti, O, and N within the graphene QDs@MOF(Ti) nanocomposites.

The crystalline structures of graphene QDs, MOF(Ti), and graphene QDs@MOF(Ti), were analyzed using XRD, as illustrated in Fig. 4 (A). The XRD spectra of MOF(Ti) were found to be consistent with previously reported results, confirming successful synthesis (Fu et al., 2021). Additionally, the XRD spectra of graphene QDs revealed a broad peak, corresponding to the (002) peak, in good agreement with the JCPDS No. 41–1487 (Kumar et al., 2017). In the case of the graphene QDs@MOF(Ti) nanocomposite, the XRD peaks of the MOF(Ti) shift towards higher 2θ values due to the disturbance in the MOF(Ti) lattice parameters caused by the QDs. The tensile stress exerted by the graphene QDs on the MOF(Ti) results in a decrease in the d-spacing of the MOF(Ti) (Khlifi et al., 2022). Further, the full XPS spectrum, shown in Fig. 4(B), indicates that the primary elements present in both samples are C, N, O, and Ti. This finding is consistent with the results from elemental mapping, confirming the composition. Fig. 4(C) shows the C1s spectrum of graphene QDs@MOF(Ti), highlighting three significant peaks at 283.5, 284.9, and 287.7 eV. These peaks correspond to the carbon-containing functional groups within the graphene QDs and MOF(Ti), specifically attributed to C=C, C–N, and C=O bonds, respectively (Shi et al., 2023). Although the C=C peak is observed at 284.9 eV slightly higher than the typical range for C=C bonds (284.4–284.5 eV) this shift can be attributed to the electron interaction between the graphene QDs and the MOF framework. The red shift in the deconvoluted C1s spectrum indicates an increase in electron density in the graphene QDs due to charge transfer from the MOF(Ti). The Ti 2p spectrum in Fig. 4(D) exhibits symmetric peaks at 457.4 and 463.3 eV, corresponding to Ti2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2, respectively (Wang et al., 2015). This indicates the presence of Ti(IV) within the titanium oxygen-cluster (Ti-O). After incorporating graphene QDs into the MOF(Ti), the Ti 2p peaks shift towards higher binding energy, indicating an increase in electron density around the Ti atoms due to the interaction between the GQDs and the MOF(Ti). The deconvoluted O1s XPS spectra of graphene QDs@MOF(Ti), shown in Fig. 4(E), display characteristic peaks at 531.36, 530.28, and 528.76 eV. These peaks correspond to oxygen atoms in the Ti-O, C=O (Wang et al., 2019), and O-H. In the deconvoluted O1s spectra, the Ti-O and O-H peaks exhibit a blue shift, while the C=O peak shows a red shift, further confirming electron redistribution within the graphene QDs@MOF(Ti) composite. Additionally, the N1s spectra, as shown in Fig. 4(F), reveals peaks at 397.8 and 401.5 eV, corresponding to N-C=O and N-H groups, respectively. Both peaks exhibit a blue shift after incorporating graphene QDs into the MOF(Ti).

The high specific surface area of a catalyst significantly enhances its adsorption and photocatalytic abilities by promoting interfacial reactions and exposing more active sites to reactants. The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm of graphene QDs@MOF(Ti) exhibits a type IV isotherm, indicating its mesoporous nature (Kumar et al., 2017). According to BET study (see Fig. 5 (A)), the specific surface areas of graphene QDs@MOF(Ti) and MOF(Ti) are 904.8 and 1134.8 m2 g−1, respectively. The notable decrease in the surface area of graphene QDs@MOF(Ti) is accredited to the presence of graphene QDs. Furthermore, Fig. 5(B) demonstrates that the pore size distribution of both graphene QDs@MOF(Ti) and MOF(Ti) remains relatively unchanged, predominantly ranging from 2 to 4 nm. Moreover, Fig. 5(C) presents the UV–vis spectra of both MOF(Ti) and graphene QDs@MOF(Ti). The results reveal an absorption peak ∼400 nm for MOF(Ti). In contrast, graphene QDs@MOF(Ti) exhibits an enhanced response not only in the visible light region but also in the near-infrared region. This improved performance is attributed to the optical absorption properties of the graphene QDs. Furthermore, based on the Tauc plot depicted in Fig. 5(D), the bandgaps were determined to be 2.59 eV for MOF(Ti) and 2.52 eV for graphene QDs@MOF(Ti). This suggests that incorporating graphene QDs onto MOF(Ti) effectively reduces its bandgap, thereby extending its light absorption range.

For comparison, conventional method is used to assess the photocatalytic degradation of both MOF(Ti) and graphene QDs@MOF(Ti). The dye solution was stirred continuously and placed in a dark environment to achieve absorption-desorption equilibrium for 30 min. Following this, visible light was then turned on for the subsequent 60 min. Throughout this period, a volume of 1.25 ml of the MB solution was collected at regular intervals of 10 min to conduct absorption measurements. Fig. S2 revealed the spectral images of a gradual reduction in the intensity of the characteristic absorption peak at 663 nm with increasing visible light exposure time. The attenuation of MB dye due to visible light exposure is evident from the inset of Figs. S2(A–C). The rate constant (k) was determined as 8.56 × 10−3 min−1, 10.1 × 10−2 min−1 and 12.9 × 10−2 min−1, derived from the slope of the linear plot of log (Co/C) versus time for the MOF(Ti), 2- and 4-graphene QDs@MOF(Ti), respectively, which affirmed the adherence to the first-order degradation kinetic model (see Figs. S2(D and E)). The experiment revealed that the degradation efficiency of the MOF(Ti) is found to be 70.32%, while the 2- and 4-graphene QDs@MOF(Ti) exhibited an enhanced degradation efficiency of 74.95% and 83.8% respectively, after 60 min of visible light irradiation (see Fig. S2(F)).

The efficacy of the photocatalyst in degrading MB dye solutions can be influenced by varying the flow rate and catalyst concentrations (Roy et al., 2022) in the microreactor. Therefore, it is important to investigate the photocatalytic capabilities of both MOF(Ti) and graphene QDs@MOF(Ti) under these situations. The initial experiment involved measuring the photocatalytic activity of MOF(Ti), and 2-graphene QDs@MOF(Ti) under visible light irradiation inside microreactor. This was done at various flow rates using a syringe pump in a continuous flow microchannel. The photocatalytic activity of both photocatalysts was measured by analyzing the absorbance of MB solution exiting the channels. Fig. 6 (A and B) demonstrates the relative decrease in MB absorption intensity as flow rates are reduced, indicating a decrease in dye concentration in the solution. The attenuation of MB dye due to visible light exposure at different flow rates is evident from the inset of corresponding Fig. 6(A and B). The relative alteration in dye concentration was determined by comparing it with the blank dye concentration using equation (3), as depicted in Fig. 6(E and F). It is important to note that the MB dye solution flowing through the microchannel exist the microreactor after 8 min at 50 μL min−1, but it takes a total of 52 min to completely fill 1.25 ml, the minimum volume required to measure the absorption of degraded MB dye. Similarly, all results also encompass the total time taken to collect 1.25 ml at different flow rates. As illustrated in Fig. 6(D), it becomes apparent that a flow rate of 50 μL min−1 yields optimal degradation of MB dye. This heightened efficiency at a lower flow rate could be ascribed to the prolonged interaction time between the photocatalyst and MB dye. Previous studies have demonstrated similar findings in research focused on contaminant degradation at low flow rates (Eskandarloo and Badiei, 2015; Meng et al., 2013; Shukla et al., 2021). Additionally, it was observed that at higher flow rates (500 μL min−1), the MOF (Ti) shows slightly better efficiency compared to the 2-graphene QDs@MOF(Ti) composite. This reduction in the composite's performance may be attributed to higher flow rates causing disturbances in the microreactor, potentially compromising the stability of the composite. Thus the 2-graphene QDs@MOF(Ti) within the microchannel can result in it being washed away with the dye solution, further lowering the degradation efficiency.

The efficiency of the photocatalytic degradation of the reactant heavily relies on the concentration of photocatalyst within the microreactor channel's wall. To quantitatively explore the impact of different concentrations of graphene QDs in MOF(Ti) on dye degradation, a comparative photocatalytic test was conducted (see Fig. 6(C)). As shown in Fig. 6(D), under the same operating conditions, the efficiency of MB degradation consistently increased with 4-graphene QDs@MOF(Ti). The shift in the overall conversion efficiency can be attributed to the heightened absorption of photons with higher concentrations of graphene QDs in MOF(Ti). This leads to an increased production of e−/h+ pairs, enabling more active participation in the degradation reactions. The experiment revealed that the degradation efficiency is significantly enhanced, reaching 98.61% under visible light irradiation at a 50 μL min−1 using 4-graphene QDs@MOF(Ti) photocatalyst.

The graphene QDs@MOF nanocomposite possess various features that enable them for the proven enhanced degradation efficiency. The abundant active sites present on porous MOF (Ti) surface, along with the graphene QDs, promotes the O2 absorption capabilities thus enhances the generation of reactive oxygen species. Additionally, the presence of graphene QDs enhances the sufficient transfer of photogenerated e−, which further magnifies the degradation ability of the composite. Fig. 7 shows the possible degradation mechanism of MB dye using graphene QDs@MOF composite. Initially, a significant quantity of MB dye molecules is adsorbed onto the surface of the graphene QDs@MOF photocatalyst upon exposure to visible light. Upon this absorption, e−/h+ pairs are generated, where the e− are excited to the CB and thereafter transferred to the graphene QDs, while the h+ remain in the valence band (VB). This pivotal step significantly enhances the efficiency of the photogenerated e−/h+ pairs, thus driving the degradation mechanism forward. Furthermore, the CB potential of the graphene QDs@MOF composite measured at −0.45 V is more negative than the reduction potential of
O 2
/
O 2
. −
(−0.33 V vs NHE) (Shi et al., 2023; Xia et al., 2017). This favorable energetic arrangement enables the photogenerated e− residing on the CB of the graphene QDs@MOF composite to effectively react with the
O 2
molecules adsorbed on the surface of the composite photocatalyst, leading to the formation of
O 2
. −
. Additionally, it is observed that the VB potential of graphene QDs@MOF, measured at 2.14 V vs NHE, slightly exceeds the oxidation potential of
OH −
/
∙ OH
(1.99 V vs NHE) (Huang et al., 2021; Yang et al., 2023). Consequently, within the reaction system, the presence of
OH −
can undergo oxidation by h+ to generate
∙ OH
(see Equations (4)–(10)).

−
+ 2
H +
→
H 2
O 2
(7)
H 2
O 2
→ 2
(
∙ O H
)
(8)
h +
+
O H
−
→ ∙ O H
(9)
h +
+
H 2
O → ∙ O H +
H +
(10)
∙ O H +
M B
a d s
→ D e g r a d e d
P r o d u c t

The degradation of MB in aqueous phase was also analyzed using comparative 1H NMR spectroscopy. 1H NMR spectrum of MB and graphene QDs@MOF(Ti) degraded MB solution (at 50 μL min−1) are shown in Fig. 8 . The presence of various protons are marked on the structure of MB dye. The 1H NMR spectra of MB exhibits a sharp singlet signal at δ 3 ppm, corresponding to aliphatic protons (designated as Ha), and three doublet signals between δ 6.7 and δ 7.5 ppm, which are attributed to aromatic protons (Hb-Hd), encompassing six distinct chemical protons. After photocatalytic treatment, the signals in the aromatic region disappear, indicating the loss of aromaticity. Additionally, the sharp peak associated with the 12 methyl protons also vanishes. New peaks emerge in the region, indicating the formation of intermediate products after photocatalytic degradation. This NMR analysis confirms the complete degradation of MB molecules, as evidenced by the disappearance of proton signals in the aromatic regions.

Moreover, assessing the reusability of the photocatalyst is crucial in determining its recyclability. Therefore, we conducted a thorough examination of 4-graphene QDs@MOF(Ti) by subjecting it to repeated degradation processes within an optofluidic microreactor. With the identical photocatalyst coating maintained within the channels, it's apparent that the initial cycle achieved a degradation efficiency of up to 98.61% at 50 μL min−1. However, subsequent cycles witnessed a gradual decline in efficiency, recording at 97.70%, 96.97% and 95.54% for the following three consecutive cycles, respectively. Consequently, even after four cycles, the degradation efficiency of MB remained only marginally reduced. This finding serves as evidence of the superior reusability exhibited by the 4-graphene QDs@MOF(Ti) photocatalyst (see Fig. 9 (A)). Additionally, the long-term stability of the 4-graphene QDs@MOF(Ti) is examined through experiments conducted over a period of 7 days (see Fig. 9(B)). Following the experiments, despite a decrease in degradation efficiency, it still persists at 87.21%, underscoring the admirable stability of the prepared 4-graphene QDs@MOF(Ti) photocatalyst.

Table 1 summarizes the comparison of previously published works on MOF(Ti) based composites for the degradation of various organic dyes. The comparative results show that the graphene QDs@MOF(Ti) decorated microreactor demonstrates superior efficiency in degrading MB dye compared to other reported MOF based photocatalysts (Ahmadi et al., 2020; Ahmadpour et al., 2020; Fu et al., 2022; Yin et al., 2020). Additionally, while recent studies have explored other reactors using photocatalysts such as ZnO, ZnS and TiO2 however, these systems tend to be more expensive to fabricate and exhibit lower degradation efficacy, and requiring more time to break down dyes (Han et al., 2013; Kim et al., 2022b; Martin et al., 2022; Prakash et al., 2020; Satuf et al., 2019). Thus the fabricated graphene QDs@MOF(Ti) decorated microreactors used in this study offer a high S/V ratio, and uniform light distribution to the photocatalyst resulting in significantly improved photocatalytic degradation performance for MB dye.

This article presents a cost-effective method for fabricating an optofluidic microreactor, featuring graphene QDs@MOF(Ti) photocatalyst decoration within the channels for MB dye degradation. Additionally, the presence of graphene QDs enhances the sufficient transfer of photogenerated e−, which further magnifies the degradation ability of the composite. The direct integration of graphene QDs@MOF(Ti) into the microchannels ensures intimate contact with the MB dye solutions, minimizing interaction time and maximizing purification efficacy of ∼99% under visible light exposure at a flow rate of 50 μL min−1. Moreover, the optofluidic microreactor showcases prolonged stability of the photocatalyst, enabling its repeated use without significant efficiency decline. These advancements underscore the superior degradation efficiency of the graphene QDs@MOF(Ti) nanocomposite, validating its remarkable potential for practical applications.

Gulshan Verma: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation, Data curation. Prashanth Venkatesan: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation, Data curation. Deblina Roy: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Investigation. Parthivi Aloni: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Investigation. Naresh Kumar Dega: Writing – review & editing, Validation. Ankur Gupta: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The author is pleased to acknowledge Ministry of Education (MoE), India, IIT Jodhpur for financial assistantship, and Environmental Chemistry & Catalysis Laboratory (National Tsing Hua University, Taiwan) for experiments and Data Analysis. The author also thanks Ms. Sakshi Bhagat (Department of Chemistry, IIT Jodhpur) for assisting in NMR characterization of untreated and treated MB dye samples.
